subtopics.title: The concept of the drainage basin as an open system

requirements.statement: The drainage basin has inputs, outputs, stores and transfers.
The relationship between inputs, outputs, stores and transfers.

subtopics.title: Movement of water through the drainage system

requirements.statement: Inputs: precipitation (rainfall, snow, hail).
Outputs: evaporation, evapotranspiration, condensation, channel flow.
Stores: atmosphere (clouds), vegetation (interception and through roots), surface (channel, lake), soil, bedrock (groundwater store below water table).
Transfers: stemflow, throughfall, infiltration, overland flow/surface runoff (Hortonian and saturation-excess overland flow), throughflow, percolation, groundwater flow/baseflow, water table.

subtopics.title: Drainage basin characteristics influencing the movement of water

requirements.statement: Size and shape of drainage basin (catchment), drainage density, porosity and permeability of soils (clay, silt, sand), rock types (granite, limestone, chalk, sandstone, clay), vegetation type, land use.
Climate: precipitation type, duration and intensity, temperature, evapotranspiration, antecedent moisture.

subtopics.title: Discharge relationships and hydrographs

requirements.statement: Annual hydrographs:
requirements.notesAndExamples: reading and interpreting variations in discharge through the year using hydrographs.; factors influencing variations in discharge through the year: climate (precipitation type, duration and intensity, temperature, evapotranspiration, antecedent moisture), vegetation type, land use.

requirements.statement: Storm hydrographs:
requirements.notesAndExamples: components of storm hydrographs: rising/falling limbs, peaks, lags, amount and timing of precipitation.; factors influencing storm hydrographs: climate (precipitation type, duration and intensity, temperature, evapotranspiration, antecedent moisture), vegetation type, land use, drainage basin characteristics.

subtopics.title: River channel processes

requirements.statement: Erosion: abrasion/corrasion, solution, cavitation, hydraulic action.
Load transport: traction, saltation, suspension, solution.
Deposition and sedimentation.
Relationship between velocity, discharge and load size.

subtopics.title: River flow

requirements.statement: Velocity and discharge, patterns of flow (laminar, turbulent, helicoidal), thalweg.

subtopics.title: Formation of river landforms by erosion and deposition processes

requirements.statement: Meanders (river cliffs, point bars, oxbow lakes), riffle and pool sequences, waterfalls, gorges, bluffs, floodplains, levées.

subtopics.title: Causes of river floods

requirements.statement: Physical factors that affect river discharge and cause flooding: duration and intensity of precipitation, snowmelt and ice melt, drainage basin characteristics.
Human land-use changes that affect river discharge and cause flooding: deforestation, changing agricultural systems and irrigation, urbanisation.

subtopics.title: Impacts of river floods

requirements.statement: Impacts on people and the environment: short-term impacts and long-term impacts.
Physical factors and human factors of vulnerability to river flood hazards and impacts: scale and duration of flood event, population density, infrastructure, economic development.

subtopics.title: Management of river floods

requirements.statement: Prediction of flood risk: recurrence intervals, hazard mapping.
Forecasts (discharge measurements at gauging stations, rainfall, snowmelt and ice melt) and warnings.
Effectiveness of hard engineering management strategies: dams, river straightening, artificial levées, diversion spillways.
Effectiveness of soft engineering management strategies: floodplain and drainage basin management, including afforestation and wetland and riverbank conservation.
Emergency responses and recovery.

Detailed specific example of one recent river flood: prediction and forecasting, causes, impacts on people and the environment and an evaluation of the success of strategies used to manage floods in this drainage basin.

topics.title: Atmospheric processes and global climate change

subtopics.title: Energy budget systems

requirements.statement: Inputs and outputs: incoming (shortwave) solar radiation, outgoing (longwave) terrestrial radiation, absorption, reflection (albedo), scattering, conduction, back radiation.
Transfers: latent heat transfer and sensible heat transfer.

subtopics.title: The global energy budget

requirements.statement: Latitudinal pattern of radiation: excesses and deficits.
Natural greenhouse effect.

subtopics.title: Global transfers of energy

requirements.statement: Atmospheric transfers: tri-cellular model of atmospheric transfer (Polar, Ferrel and Hadley cells, intertropical convergence zone (ITCZ)), wind belts, jet streams.
Oceanic transfers: vertical and horizontal transfers by ocean currents and ocean gyres.
Relationships between atmospheric transfers and oceanic transfers.

subtopics.title: Seasonal variations in temperature, pressure and wind belts

requirements.statement: Influence of latitude, land/sea distribution, ocean currents.

subtopics.title: Diurnal energy budgets

requirements.statement: Differences between daytime and nighttime.

subtopics.title: Atmospheric moisture processes

requirements.statement: Evaporation, condensation, freezing, melting, sublimation.

subtopics.title: Causes of precipitation

requirements.statement: Convection, frontal and orographic uplift of air, radiation cooling.

subtopics.title: Types of precipitation

requirements.statement: Clouds, rain, hail, snow, dew, fog.

subtopics.title: Evidence for global warming and climate change

requirements.statement: Proxy evidence for past climatic conditions: ice cores, tree rings, fossils.
Evidence of recent atmospheric and sea temperature increases:
requirements.notesAndExamples: recorded temperatures since 1850.; the link between temperature records (pre- and post-1850) and carbon dioxide emissions.

requirements.statement: Other evidence of global warming: sea-level rise, sea ice reduction, ice sheet and glacial shrinkage.

subtopics.title: Causes of global warming and climate change

requirements.statement: Greenhouse gases (GHG) (carbon dioxide, methane, nitrous oxide, F-gases):
requirements.notesAndExamples: Global Warming Potential (GWP).; longevity in the atmosphere.; relative concentration in the atmosphere.; carbon dioxide equivalence (CO2 equivalence).

requirements.statement: Physical factors that affect global energy budgets:
requirements.notesAndExamples: longer-term impacts: solar output.; shorter-term impacts: volcanic eruptions.; other impacts: El Niño Southern Oscillation (ENSO).

requirements.statement: Human (anthropogenic) factors that affect global energy budgets:
requirements.notesAndExamples: activities causing greenhouse gas emissions.; activities causing changes to greenhouse gas absorption and storage.; impacts on reflection (albedo).

requirements.statement: Significance of the impact of the enhanced greenhouse effect on global energy budgets.

subtopics.title: Tectonic plates

requirements.statement: Continental plates and oceanic plates: density, age, thickness and composition.
Relative directions and rates of plate movement.

subtopics.title: Tectonic movement

requirements.statement: Continental drift theory and the ‘jigsaw’ fit of continents.
Geological and fossil records.
Evidence for sea floor spreading: Earth’s magnetic field recorded in rocks (palaeomagnetism), the age of sea floor rocks.
Convection currents, slab pull, ridge push.

subtopics.title: Global distribution of types of plate boundaries

requirements.statement: Divergent/constructive.
Convergent/destructive.
Convergent/collision.
Conservative/transform.

subtopics.title: Tectonic processes

requirements.statement: Faulting and rifting at divergent/constructive boundaries.
Sea floor spreading at divergent/constructive boundaries.
Subduction at convergent/destructive boundaries.
Shearing and thrusting at convergent/collision boundaries.
Faulting at conservative/transform boundaries.

subtopics.title: Formation of landforms by tectonic processes:

requirements.statement: Fold mountains, volcanoes, mid-ocean ridges, ocean trenches, volcanic island arcs, fault scarps, rift valleys.

subtopics.title: Types of mass movement

requirements.statement: Heaves.
Flows (mudflow, debris flow).
Slides (landslides, rotational slides/slumps).
Falls (rock).

subtopics.title: Physical causes and human causes of mass movement

requirements.statement: Physical causes: geology, shape of the land (topography), precipitation intensity and amounts, potential impact of climate change.
Human causes: building and road construction, land-use change, water management.

subtopics.title: Management of mass movement hazards

requirements.statement: Prediction and risk identification of mass movements (hazard mapping and monitoring).
Impacts on people and the environment.
Management strategies to improve slope stability:
requirements.notesAndExamples: pinning, netting.; slope grading, slope drainage.; afforestation.; planning restrictions on building and development.

requirements.statement: Emergency responses and recovery.

Detailed specific example of one recent mass movement event: prediction, causes, impacts on people and the environment and an evaluation of the success of strategies used to manage mass movement events in conditions similar to the event studied. Paper 2 – Human Geography Candidates must study the following three topics. See section 4 for Details of the Assessment for Paper 2. For further information on the use of examples and detailed specific examples see page 14. These topics develop an understanding of different aspects of human environments. They develop understanding from IGCSE/O Level Geography and consider the various factors (physical, environmental, social, economic, political, historical) influencing these human environments, current and future challenges and opportunities for these human environments.

subtopics.title: Population distribution and population density

requirements.statement: Current patterns of global population distribution.
Factors (physical, environmental, social, economic, political, historical) influencing population density.

subtopics.title: Trends in global population growth and regional variations

requirements.statement: Factors (physical, environmental, social, economic, political, historical) affecting global population growth.
Current trends in global population growth.
Factors (physical, environmental, social, economic, political, historical) influencing regional variations in population growth.

subtopics.title: Components of population change and their spatial variations

requirements.statement: Natural increase and net migration: birth rate and death rate, total fertility rate (TFR), infant mortality rate (IMR), life expectancy.
Factors (social, economic, environmental, political) affecting levels of fertility and mortality.

subtopics.title: Population structures

requirements.statement: Components of population structure (age, gender, dependency, dependency ratio).
Interpretation of different age/sex structure diagrams.

subtopics.title: Changes in population structures over time

requirements.statement: Factors influencing population structure: natural increase and net migration.
Critical evaluation of the demographic transition model (DTM): purpose, limitations, usefulness when applied to a range of countries.
Youthful population structures and ageing population structures: environmental, social, economic, political impacts.

subtopics.title: Government attempts to manage natural increase

requirements.statement: Reasons why governments attempt to manage the consequences of natural increase.
Examples of population policies in two contrasting countries.
Relative success of one country’s attempt to manage natural increase.

subtopics.title: International migration types

requirements.statement: Voluntary: economic, social.
Non-voluntary (forced): asylum seekers and refugees.
Temporary and permanent.

subtopics.title: Causes of migration

requirements.statement: Push and pull factors: environmental, economic, cultural, demographic, political.
Constraints (obstacles and barriers) to migration: physical, environmental, social, economic, political.

subtopics.title: Impacts of migration

requirements.statement: Positive and negative impacts (environmental, social, economic, political, demographic) on:
requirements.notesAndExamples: source areas.; receiving/destination areas.; migrants.

requirements.statement: Factors influencing level of impact:
requirements.notesAndExamples: source area characteristics: population structure, income classification of country, level of education and skill of population.; migrant characteristics: age, ability to finance travel, level of education and skill.; migration flow: number of migrants, type of migration flow.; receiving/destination area characteristics: population structure, income classification of country, level of education and skill of population.

requirements.statement: Detailed specific example of the impacts of international migration on one country: the causes, characteristics, scale, pattern and an evaluation of attempts at managing international migration.

subtopics.title: Patterns of water resources, spatial variation and changes over time

requirements.statement: Types of water resources: rivers, lakes and reservoirs, oceans, underground water, ice sheets, precipitation, recycled.
Global pattern of water resources.

subtopics.title: The human water cycle

requirements.statement: Capture, management, use, disposal, reuse of water.
Modification to the water cycle: agriculture, deforestation and afforestation, urbanisation, industrialisation, water abstraction, flood management.

subtopics.title: Trends in water consumption

requirements.statement: Water stress and water scarcity (physical and economic).
Water consumption linked to economic development.
Changing demands for water from human activities: agriculture, domestic use, different industrial sectors.

subtopics.title: Factors that influence the supply of water (volume and quality)

requirements.statement: Physical factors affecting water resources: climate, geology, drainage patterns, relief.
Human factors affecting water resources: storage and water transfers, water abstraction, salt removal (desalination), water treatment and recycling, type of infrastructure.

subtopics.title: Factors that influence the demand for water

requirements.statement: Population characteristics: number and income characteristics.
Economic structure: type of manufacturing industry, agriculture, recreation and tourism.
Seasonal demand.

subtopics.title: Water security

requirements.statement: The concept of water security.
Global pattern of water security and water insecurity.
Physical factors and human factors that contribute to water insecurity.
Water access issues: poverty, pollution, infrastructure.
Impact of water insecurity and drought on people and the environment.

subtopics.title: Strategies to increase water resources and access to water

requirements.statement: Relative success of strategies: dams and reservoirs, multipurpose river schemes, water transfers, salt removal (desalination), rainwater harvesting, use of grey water, technological solutions for areas without piped and clean water supplies.

subtopics.title: Strategies to manage demand for water

requirements.statement: Relative success of strategies: increasing the price, rationing, technology to reduce amount needed, publicity/education on water conservation.

subtopics.title: Management of water resources

requirements.statement: Variability in water resources (volume and quality) and impacts: environmental, social, economic, political.
Challenges of managing water resources: rates of abstraction, saltwater intrusion, water conflicts, transboundary issues, water pollution, ground subsidence.
Detailed specific example from one country: the challenges in managing water resources, the strategies used and evaluation of the relative success of these strategies.

subtopics.title: Processes of urban growth

requirements.statement: Growth in population and area: globally and at city level.
Cities experiencing different types of urban growth: urbanisation, suburbanisation, urban sprawl, counter- urbanisation, re-urbanisation, urban renewal and regeneration.

subtopics.title: Causes and consequences of urban growth for urban areas and rural areas

requirements.statement: Causes of urban growth: social, economic, political, historical.
Consequences of urban growth for urban areas and rural areas (environmental, social, economic, political).

subtopics.title: Urban growth and a hierarchy of urban areas

requirements.statement: The concept of a hierarchy of urban areas in a country.
Primate cities: causes and consequences for countries.
World cities: categories and causes.

subtopics.title: Factors which influence the structure and characteristics of urban areas

requirements.statement: Horizontal versus vertical urban structures and urban land-use zones.
The role of factors (physical, social, economic, political, historical) in creating distinct structures and patterns of land use.
The concept of place identity and perception of place.

subtopics.title: The changing location of urban activities

requirements.statement: Factors (population change, cost of land, transport provision, competition for space, investment, local and national planning) causing changes in the characteristics and location of: central business district (CBD), retail, manufacturing, services, residential activities.
Rate of change may vary in different cities depending on the location, economic classification and influences on change.

subtopics.title: Zonation of residential areas

requirements.statement: Characteristics of different residential areas.
Causes of residential zonation: income, supply of housing, ethnicity, planning, cultural and historical factors.

subtopics.title: Challenges of sustainability in urban development

requirements.statement: The concept of sustainability.
Difficulties in measuring sustainability.

Balance between environmental, social, economic and political priorities and the view of different groups of people (residents, landowners, local business owners, community groups, local and national government).

subtopics.title: Sustainability issues in urban areas

requirements.statement: Solid waste disposal, transport (public and private) and quality and density of housing.
Role of green space and water features.

subtopics.title: Strategies for the sustainable management of urban areas

requirements.statement: Strategies for reducing the issues of solid waste disposal, pollution, transport and housing.
Constraints on and incentives to successful management of urban areas.

Detailed specific example from one urban area: the challenges in sustainable management, the strategy or scheme used, and evaluation of the relative success of the strategy or scheme. Candidates study four topics. The order of topics in the syllabus is not a recommended teaching order. Topics can be taught in any order. Through studying these topics, candidates will further develop the skills acquired in studying the AS Level topics. Candidates will be expected to be able to show the following skills:
An ability to use and apply their knowledge and understanding to construct their own explanations and arguments.
An ability to understand the role of place and interdependence in creating different outcomes, different viewpoints and to make evaluations regarding the relative success or failure of initiatives.
An ability to interpret and evaluate information and produce reasoned conclusions.

An ability to present a logical, structured, coherent and evidence-based argument. These skills provide a solid foundation for progression to university. Teachers are expected to integrate the teaching of geographical skills through their delivery of the subject content. Some resource types will be relevant to several topics, other resource types will be particularly relevant to one topic. Definitions will not be assessed. Paper 3 – Global Environments In Paper 3, candidates choose two topics from:
Topic 7 Tropical environments
Topic 8 Coastal environments
Topic 9 Hazardous environments

Topic 10 Arid environments See section 4 for Details of the Assessment for Paper 3. For further information on the use of examples and detailed specific examples see page 14. These topics develop an understanding of different global environments. These topics build on the foundational understanding from the AS Level topics to consider the physical factors and processes that produce the diverse features of these global environments. Each topic also considers the interdependence of physical systems and the dynamic interaction between people and the environment, how human activities impact these global environments and the consequences of these impacts.

subtopics.title: Global distribution of humid tropical (rainforest) and seasonally humid tropical (savanna) environments
Climatic characteristics of humid tropical (rainforest) and seasonally humid tropical (savanna) environments including monsoons

requirements.statement: Key features of temperature and precipitation.
Annual variations and diurnal variations in temperature and precipitation.

subtopics.title: Reasons for the global distribution and climatic characteristics of humid tropical (rainforest) and seasonally humid tropical (savanna) environments

requirements.statement: Roles of the intertropical convergence zone (ITCZ), subtropical anticyclones, and seasonal monsoons.
Influence of El Niño Southern Oscillation (ENSO).

subtopics.title: Weathering processes and their significance in forming and shaping landforms

requirements.statement: Chemical weathering processes: hydrolysis, carbonation.
Physical (mechanical) weathering processes: pressure release (dilatation), salt crystal growth.
Biological weathering: chelation, vegetation root growth.

subtopics.title: Granite landforms: bornhardts (dwala, ruware), kopjes (koppies), tors

requirements.statement: Characteristics and formation: the role of weathering, climate, rock structure.

subtopics.title: Limestone landforms: tropical karst – cone karst, tower karst, cockpit karst

requirements.statement: Distribution, characteristics and formation: the role of weathering, climate, rock structure.

subtopics.title: Vegetation characteristics of rainforest ecosystems and savanna ecosystems

requirements.statement: Development of plant communities: climatic climax, subclimax and plagioclimax.
Impact of climate and human activities.

subtopics.title: Soils in rainforests and savannas

requirements.statement: Distinctive soil forming processes.
Soil types and profile characteristics: oxisols/latosols, tropical red and brown earths.
Impact of climate, vegetation and human activities.

subtopics.title: Nutrient cycling in rainforests and savannas

requirements.statement: Gersmehl diagrams, soil fertility, energy flows and trophic levels.
Impact of climate, vegetation and human activities (burning, clearing and deforestation).

subtopics.title: Changes and challenges and their impacts on tropical environments:

requirements.statement: Increase in population pressure and changes in land use.
Threats from exploitation: large-scale monoculture, logging, mining.
Challenges associated with climate change: changes in temperature and precipitation, increase in wildfires.

subtopics.title: Detailed specific example of one tropical ecosystem (either the rainforest ecosystem or the savanna ecosystem):

requirements.statement: Issues affecting the ecosystem.
Management strategies.
Evaluation of the success of these management strategies.

subtopics.title: Factors influencing coastal environments

requirements.statement: Wave generation and wave characteristics:
requirements.notesAndExamples: fetch, high and low energy waves, wave refraction, breaking waves, swash and backwash.

requirements.statement: Rock type and structure.
Climate.
Human activities.

subtopics.title: Marine erosion processes

requirements.statement: Hydraulic action, cavitation, corrasion, abrasion, attrition.

subtopics.title: Sub-aerial processes

requirements.statement: Weathering:
requirements.notesAndExamples: physical (mechanical) weathering: freeze–thaw, salt crystal growth.; chemical weathering: carbonation, solution, hydrolysis.

requirements.statement: Mass movement: rockfalls, landslides, mudflows.

subtopics.title: Marine transportation and marine deposition processes

requirements.statement: Traction, saltation, suspension, longshore drift.
Sediment sources, sediment characteristics and sediment cells.

subtopics.title: The role and significance of coastal processes in forming and shaping characteristic landforms.
Erosional landforms

requirements.statement: Cliffs (cross-section and plan), shore platforms, caves, geos, arches, stacks.
Concordant and discordant coastlines.

subtopics.title: Depositional landforms

requirements.statement: Beaches (cross-section and plan).
Swash-aligned and drift-aligned beaches.
Spits.
Tombolos.
Barrier beaches.

subtopics.title: Formation and development of landforms influenced by coastal processes and vegetation types

requirements.statement: Coastal sand dune systems.
Coastal saltmarsh systems.
Mangrove swamps.

subtopics.title: Distribution of coral reefs and conditions required for coral growth

requirements.statement: Temperature, water depth, light and lack of sediment, water oxygenation, salinity.
How these factors affect coral growth.
Symbiotic relationship with algae (zooxanthellae).

subtopics.title: Threats to coral reefs

requirements.statement: Global warming: marine temperature rise, marine acidification, increased storm activity, changes in salinity, sea-level rise.
Pollution: marine and land based.
Physical damage to reef structure: caused by coastal processes and storms, human activities.
Management strategies to reduce threats to coral reefs and evaluation of the success in reducing threats to coral reefs.

subtopics.title: Detailed specific example of a stretch or stretches of coastline:

requirements.statement: Issues faced in the stretch or stretches of coastline.
Management strategies (including hard engineering and soft engineering).
Evaluation of the success of these management strategies.

subtopics.title: Global distribution of earthquakes and volcanoes and related tectonic processes and plate boundaries
Earthquake hazards and impacts

requirements.statement: Factors affecting severity of earthquakes:
requirements.notesAndExamples: focus and epicentre.; types of seismic waves: P-waves and S-waves, Love waves and Rayleigh waves.; magnitude and magnitude scales: Richter, Modified Mercalli and Moment Magnitude.

requirements.statement: Main hazards: ground-shaking, liquefaction, landslides, tsunami, aftershocks.
Physical factors and human factors of vulnerability to earthquake hazards and impacts: significance of time of day, distance from epicentre, population density, infrastructure, economic development.
Impacts on people and the environment: short-term impacts and long-term impacts.

subtopics.title: Volcanic hazards and impacts

requirements.statement: Processes involved in volcano formation.
Types of volcano: strato-volcano (composite cone), shield volcano, cinder cone, lava dome, fissure eruptions.
Explosive and effusive eruptions and their main hazards: lava flows, tephra and ash falls, volcanic mudflows/lahars, volcanic landslides, pyroclastic flows (nuées ardentes), jökulhlaups, toxic gases.
Volcanic explosivity index (VEI).
Physical factors and human factors of vulnerability to volcanic hazards and impacts: speed, size, frequency, extent of the eruption, population density, infrastructure, economic development.
Impacts on people and the environment: short-term impacts and long-term impacts.

subtopics.title: Management of earthquake and volcanic hazards

requirements.statement: Prediction techniques and their reliability, precursor events (warning signs) and warning times.
The concept of perception of risk.
Effectiveness of management strategies:
requirements.notesAndExamples: environmental and infrastructure design or modification.; planning, education and awareness.

requirements.statement: Short-term emergency responses and long-term recovery.

subtopics.title: Global distribution of tropical cyclones (cyclones, hurricanes, typhoons)

requirements.statement: Conditions required for tropical cyclones: sea temperature, seasonality, Coriolis effect, local atmospheric conditions.
Formation and development of tropical cyclones.
Saffir-Simpson (hurricane) scale of tropical cyclone strength.

subtopics.title: Tropical cyclone hazards and impacts

requirements.statement: Main hazards: high winds, storm surges, coastal flooding causing saltwater intrusion, intense rainfall causing river floods and mass movements.

Physical factors and human factors of vulnerability to tropical cyclone hazards and impacts: strength and extent of tropical cyclone, location of landfall, population density, infrastructure, economic development.
Impacts on people and the environment: short-term impacts and long-term impacts.
Potential impacts of climate change on the magnitude and frequency of tropical cyclones.

subtopics.title: Management of tropical cyclone hazards

requirements.statement: Prediction techniques and their reliability, precursor events (warning signs), monitoring and warning times.
The concept of perception of risk.
Effectiveness of management strategies:
requirements.notesAndExamples: environmental and infrastructure design or modification.; planning, education and awareness.

requirements.statement: Short-term emergency responses and long-term recovery.

subtopics.title: Global distribution of areas at risk of wildfires

requirements.statement: Conditions influencing wildfire risk: fuel (vegetation type, flammability and density), climatic factors, recent weather conditions.
Physical causes: desiccation of vegetation, lightning strikes, sparks from falling rocks.
Human causes:
requirements.notesAndExamples: accidental occurrence: unattended fires, equipment use/malfunction, rubbish (lenses/glass, discarded cigarettes).; deliberate actions: arson.

subtopics.title: Wildfire hazards and impacts

requirements.statement: Wildfire characteristics, speed and extent of wildfire spread, the influence of the shape of the land (topography) and wind.
Physical factors and human factors of vulnerability to wildfire hazards and impacts: speed and extent of spread, land use, population density and spread, infrastructure, economic development.
Impacts on people and the environment: short-term impacts and long-term impacts.
Potential impacts of climate change on the location, extent and frequency of wildfires.

subtopics.title: Management of wildfire hazards

requirements.statement: Prediction techniques and their reliability, monitoring and warning times.
The concept of perception of risk.
Effectiveness of management strategies:
requirements.notesAndExamples: environmental and infrastructure design or modification.; planning, education and awareness.

requirements.statement: Short-term emergency responses and long-term recovery.

subtopics.title: Detailed specific example of one environment which has multiple hazards (two or more of the following hazards: earthquake, volcanic, tropical cyclones, wildfires):

requirements.statement: Causes and impacts of the hazards.
Management strategies.
Evaluation of the success of these management strategies.

subtopics.title: Global distribution of hot arid and hot semi-arid environments
Climatic characteristics of hot arid and hot semi-arid environments

requirements.statement: High-wind energy environments.
Diurnal variations and seasonal variations in temperature and precipitation.

subtopics.title: Causes of aridity

requirements.statement: Pressure and wind systems.
Distance from the sea (continentality).
Influence of ocean currents.
Rain shadow effect.

subtopics.title: Weathering processes and their significance in forming and shaping landforms

requirements.statement: Physical (mechanical) weathering processes: heating/cooling (thermal fracture and exfoliation), freeze–thaw (frost shattering), salt crystal growth.
Chemical weathering processes: hydrolysis, carbonation.

subtopics.title: Wind (aeolian) processes and their significance in forming and shaping landforms

requirements.statement: Erosion: abrasion/corrasion, deflation.
Transportation: traction, saltation, suspension.
Deposition.

subtopics.title: Water (fluvial) processes and their significance in forming and shaping landforms

requirements.statement: Erosion, transportation and deposition.
Hydrological regime, episodic rainfall, sheet floods and flash floods.

subtopics.title: Characteristics, formation and shaping of landforms:

requirements.statement: Wind erosional landforms: deflation hollows, ventifacts, rock pedestals, yardang, zeugen.
Wind depositional landforms: sand dunes – seif, star, barchan.
Water erosional landforms: wadis/arroyos, pediments, mesas, buttes, inselbergs.
Water depositional landforms: alluvial fans, bajadas, playas, salt lakes.

subtopics.title: Vegetation characteristics in hot arid and hot semi-arid environments

requirements.statement: Biomass productivity: limited biodiversity, limited nutrient cycling.
Adaptation of plants to extreme temperatures.
Adaptation of plants to physical and physiological drought.
Impact of climate and human activities.

subtopics.title: Soils in hot arid and hot semi-arid environments

requirements.statement: Distinctive soil forming processes.
Characteristics of arid soils: solonetz, solonchaks.
Impact of climate, vegetation and human activities.

subtopics.title: Changes and challenges and their impacts on arid environments:

requirements.statement: Increase in population pressure and changes in land use.
Desertification (both physical factors and human factors) causing the degradation of soils (salinisation) and vegetation in hot semi-arid environments.
Challenges associated with climate change: changes in temperature and precipitation, threats to agriculture.

subtopics.title: Detailed specific example of one arid environment (either a hot arid or a hot semi-arid environment):

requirements.statement: Issues affecting the arid environment.
Management strategies.
Evaluation of the success of these management strategies. Paper 4 – Global Themes In Paper 4, candidates choose two topics from:
Topic 11 Climate change impacts and governance
Topic 12 Environmental issues and management
Topic 13 Trade, aid and tourism

Topic 14 Disease and geography See section 4 for Details of the Assessment for Paper 4. For further information on the use of examples and detailed specific examples see page 14. These topics develop an understanding of the diverse and dynamic factors influencing global themes. These topics build on the foundational understanding from the AS Level topics to consider the various factors (physical, environmental, social, economic, political, historical) influencing these global themes, how the effects of global themes may vary between countries and an understanding of the diversity of responses to global themes.

subtopics.title: Climate projections and the impact on the global climate

requirements.statement: Five categories of projected climate change: very high, high, intermediate, low and very low (with very high relating to the largest amount of global warming and very low relating to the smallest amount of global warming).
Understanding what the five categories mean in terms of:
requirements.notesAndExamples: projected amount of global warming (in °C above pre-industrial average) by 2100.; global changes in projected greenhouse gas (GHG) (carbon dioxide, methane, nitrous oxide, F-gases) emissions: increasing emissions, maintaining emissions, emissions rising to a peak then reducing.; the year that greenhouse gas emissions rise to a peak, if applicable.; the year of net zero greenhouse gas emissions, if applicable.; the concept of net zero: meaning and importance. continued

subtopics.title: Climate change impacts on different environments

requirements.statement: Cold environments (high-latitude areas, i.e. the Arctic and the Antarctic regions, and high-altitude areas): rise in temperature, reduction in snow and ice cover, permafrost melt and methane release.
Marine environments: sea-level rise and coastal flooding, changes in sea surface temperature, changes to global ocean circulation.

subtopics.title: Non-linear change in climate

requirements.statement: Feedback loops (mechanisms): positive feedback (increasing change), negative feedback (reducing change).
The concept of a climate tipping point.
Projected tipping points related to sea ice and ice sheet loss, permafrost melt and ocean circulation collapse.
How these tipping points might impact the magnitude and speed of change in cold environments, marine environments and atmospheric environments.
Interactions between cold environments, marine environments and atmospheric environments.

subtopics.title: Global socio-economic impacts and their distribution

requirements.statement: Current and future projected potential impacts:
requirements.notesAndExamples: areas of human habitation.; food production.; property (land and buildings). continued

subtopics.title: Patterns of human vulnerability to climate change

requirements.statement: Factors influencing human vulnerability to climate change:
requirements.notesAndExamples: environmental factors related to locational vulnerability: low-lying areas, areas vulnerable to storms, areas vulnerable to drought/flooding.; historical, political and economic factors.; reliance on sensitive ecosystems.

requirements.statement: How a combination of these factors can cause increased vulnerability.
Locations and people defined by the IPCC as most vulnerable to climate change:
requirements.notesAndExamples: least-developed countries (LDCs).; small-scale food producers.; low-income households.; tropical and subtropical small-island communities.; indigenous communities. Note: This sub-topic considers the impacts of climate change. When discussing the countries and communities defined as most vulnerable by the IPCC teachers should also consider sub-topic 11.3.2 for management strategies and consider the resilience of these countries and communities and the management strategies available to them.

subtopics.title: Governance on a global scale

requirements.statement: An overview of the Paris Agreement (COP21 in 2015) to limit the increase in the global average temperature.
Major agreements made at COP events since 2015.

subtopics.title: Progress and challenges in management of climate change

requirements.statement: Management of climate change to reduce the amount of change or limit the impacts of climate change (mitigation strategies) and adapting to the impacts of climate change (adaptation strategies).
Mitigation strategies aiming to get to net zero emissions:
requirements.notesAndExamples: targets for sustainable energy production and energy use.; carbon offsetting and carbon credit.; climate engineering: carbon capture and storage (CCS), solar radiation management.; nature-based solutions to increase carbon storage and improve resilience of ecosystems: protection, restoration, improvements.

requirements.statement: Adaptation strategies: withdrawal (retreat), compromise (accommodation), protection.
Limitations on choices of mitigation and adaptation strategies and challenges of implementing strategies.

subtopics.title: Detailed specific examples of two countries with contrasting management strategies:

requirements.statement: Factors influencing the choice of management strategies implemented by these countries (including any justice issues).
Evaluation of the challenges of implementing these management strategies.

subtopics.title: Energy resources

requirements.statement: Types of energy used to produce electricity, and their uses and issues:
requirements.notesAndExamples: renewable energy sources: wind, solar, tidal, hydroelectric power (HEP), geothermal, biomass (solid, liquid, gaseous forms).; non-renewable fossil fuels: coal, gas, oil.; nuclear.

requirements.statement: Global patterns and trends in energy production and consumption.
Other sources of domestic energy (combustible fuels): wood, charcoal, animal dung.

subtopics.title: Challenges of energy transition from fossil fuels to sustainable energy sources

requirements.statement: Environmental, social, economic, and political factors influencing sustainable energy production.
Scale and efficiency of sustainable energy production.
Storage of energy and connectivity to energy grids at different scales.
Decarbonisation of manufacturing industry.

subtopics.title: Factors at the national scale (environmental, social, economic, political) affecting demand and supply and the balance between different sources of energy

requirements.statement: Resource endowment, climate, pollution, income characteristics, investment in energy technology, balance of types of economic activity, sustainability, energy policy, energy security.

subtopics.title: Detailed specific examples of two contrasting countries’ electrical energy production and sources of energy at the national scale:

requirements.statement: Factors influencing the choice of sources of energy used to produce electrical energy.
Challenges of providing energy security.
Evaluation of the success of the strategies to supply electrical energy.

subtopics.title: Human activities that cause large-scale (major) pollution (land, air, water) and degradation of rural environments

requirements.statement: Rock and mineral extraction (mining and quarrying).
Agricultural practices and related issues.
Large-scale infrastructure projects.

subtopics.title: Management of pollution and environmental degradation caused by human activities

requirements.statement: Sustainable practices in rock and mineral extraction:
requirements.notesAndExamples: strategies for restoring extractive landscapes.; the role of monitoring and legislation.

requirements.statement: Sustainable environmental practices in agriculture:
requirements.notesAndExamples: practices which conserve resources, improve soil health, encourage and protect biodiversity.; sustainable strategies in agriculture. continued

subtopics.title: Detailed specific example of environmental degradation in one rural area:

requirements.statement: Causes and issues.
Attempts at management.
Evaluation of the strategies to manage the causes and issues and restore the degraded environment.

subtopics.title: Factors influencing the pollution (land, air, water) and environmental degradation of urban environments

requirements.statement: Characteristics of the urban environment: building density, high population, transport networks, concentration of economic activities, lack of open spaces.
Similarities and differences between the main causes of urban degradation in LICs, MICs and HICs.
Sources of pollution:
requirements.notesAndExamples: domestic.; construction.; manufacturing industry and power generation.; transport.; waste (solid and sewage waste).

subtopics.title: Management of pollution and environmental degradation in urban areas

requirements.statement: Sustainable practices in:
requirements.notesAndExamples: domestic environments.; construction.; energy conservation.; small scale, on-site power generation.; transport.; waste management.

subtopics.title: The reasons for urban planning and controls
Detailed specific example of environmental degradation in one urban area:

requirements.statement: Causes and issues.
Attempts at management.
Evaluation of the strategies to manage the causes and issues and restore the degraded environment.

subtopics.title: Flows and patterns of visible trade (commodities and goods) between importing countries and exporting countries

requirements.statement: Between types of countries and trade blocs.
Major trade routes.
Primary commodities (agricultural, minerals including oil and gas).
Manufactured goods (components, finished products).
Inequalities in volume and value of trade.

subtopics.title: Factors influencing patterns of visible trade (commodities and goods) between importing countries and exporting countries

requirements.statement: Physical and historical factors:
requirements.notesAndExamples: geographical location: locational advantage, resource endowment.; the impact of colonialism in establishing trade patterns and the ongoing influence on trade of many post-colonial countries.

requirements.statement: Economic and political factors:
requirements.notesAndExamples: changes in the supply of and demand for commodities.; developments in logistics: transport infrastructure and information and communication technology (ICT).; financial resources and infrastructure.; workforce structure and characteristics.; the role of regional trade blocs, bilateral agreements, protectionist policies and price setting.; the role of transnational corporations (TNCs) in global production networks including TNCs from newly emerging economies (NEEs).; level of development.

subtopics.title: Detailed specific examples of two contrasting countries (one of which has a dependence on export of primary commodities) comparison of:

requirements.statement: Commodities/goods traded by these countries.
Benefits of global trade for these countries.
Challenges of global trade for these countries.

subtopics.title: The role and impact of inter-governmental organisations (IGOs) on global trade flows

requirements.statement: The World Trade Organization (WTO) and United Nations Conference on Trade and Development (UNCTAD):
requirements.notesAndExamples: in promoting trade between countries.; dealing with issues and disputes.; monitoring and promoting free trade and trade facilitation measures.; protecting intellectual property rights.; critical evaluation of the issues of the Aid for Trade initiative. continued

subtopics.title: International aid and the role of non-governmental organisations (NGOs) and inter-governmental organisations (IGOs)

requirements.statement: Different types of international aid and aid donors:
requirements.notesAndExamples: humanitarian aid.; development aid.; climate aid.; tied aid.; bilateral aid and multilateral aid.

requirements.statement: Critical evaluation of international aid for donor countries and receiving countries.

subtopics.title: Patterns and trends in international tourism

requirements.statement: Patterns include:
requirements.notesAndExamples: characteristics of international tourists.; sources of international tourists and tourist destinations.; seasonality.; volume of tourism.; value of tourism (a type of invisible trade).

requirements.statement: Factors influencing patterns include:
requirements.notesAndExamples: developments in transport and ICT.; affordability.; TNC involvement in hotel and resort development.; government support for tourism as an economic policy.

requirements.statement: Trends in different types of tourism: business, recreational and sport, seasonal, medical, ecotourism, cultural and heritage.

subtopics.title: Impacts of tourism

requirements.statement: Economic, social, and environmental impacts of tourism:
requirements.notesAndExamples: tourism carrying capacity.

requirements.statement: Issues of sustainability including climate change:
requirements.notesAndExamples: the balance between the economic, social and environmental impacts of tourism.; sustainability considerations for visitors: carbon cost of travel, environmental claims made by destinations and resorts.

subtopics.title: Detailed specific examples of two contrasting tourist areas or resorts (one of which attracts a large number of international tourists):

requirements.statement: Development of tourism.
Impacts of tourism.
Evaluation of the success of attempts to manage tourism in these two tourist areas or resorts.

subtopics.title: Geography of disease

requirements.statement: Categorisations for the proportion of a particular population infected (prevalence) and geographical spread: disease outbreak, epidemic, pandemic (limited to a world region or global), endemic.
Social measures of disease impact: morbidity (people with a disease), death rate, infant mortality rate (IMR), life expectancy, with reference to age/sex structure diagrams.
Economic measures of disease impact: gross domestic product (GDP), spending on healthcare, workforce issues.

subtopics.title: Distribution of disease and spatial variation in morbidity

requirements.statement: Similarities and differences between main causes of death (as reported by the World Health Organization (WHO)) for LICs, MICs and HICs.
Factors influencing spatial variation in health and morbidity:
requirements.notesAndExamples: demographic variations.; environmental: climate, water quality and quantity, environmental conditions that disease carrying insects/animals (vectors) need, sanitation and healthcare infrastructure.; the impact of disaster events: flooding, tropical cyclones, earthquakes.; social: living conditions, diet, education, behaviours (smoking, exercise).; economic: poverty/affluence affecting diet, lifestyle and health, occupation, economic development, investment in healthcare infrastructure, vulnerability of infrastructure.; political: governance, investment, international relations.; global interconnectedness: migration, travel.

subtopics.title: Influenza (flu) as an example of a viral disease Note: Candidates do not need to know details of different types of human influenza (flu). They should have

requirements.statement: Transmission, threat to populations and distribution:
requirements.notesAndExamples: how influenza (flu) is transmitted and factors influencing its spread.; prevalence and risk factors of influenza (flu).; global distribution and spatial and time variation of influenza (flu).; social and economic impacts of influenza (flu).

requirements.statement: Risks associated with new human strains of influenza (flu), including potential of new variants from animal populations.
Detailed specific example of the strategies used by one country to prevent and control influenza (flu).

subtopics.title: Cholera as an example of a bacterial disease

requirements.statement: Transmission, threat to populations and distribution:
requirements.notesAndExamples: how cholera is transmitted and factors influencing its spread.; prevalence and risk factors of cholera.; global distribution and spatial variation of cholera.; the potential risks of cholera spread following disaster events.; social and economic impacts of cholera.

requirements.statement: Detailed specific example of the strategies used by one country to prevent and control cholera.

subtopics.title: Malaria as an example of a parasitic disease spread by a vector (mosquito)

requirements.statement: Transmission, threat to populations and distribution:
requirements.notesAndExamples: how malaria is transmitted and factors influencing its spread.; prevalence and risk factors of malaria.; global distribution and spatial variation of malaria and spread due to changing environmental conditions.; social and economic impacts of malaria.

requirements.statement: Detailed specific example of the strategies used by one country to prevent and control malaria.

subtopics.title: Monitoring disease to manage public health emergencies

requirements.statement: Factors that influence effective disease monitoring: cost-effective testing, access and scalability of testing technology, access to patients, data accuracy, disclosure and patient confidentiality, international relations (collaboration).
Controls of disease: vaccines, education, sanitation infrastructure, low-tech solutions (mosquito nets, face masks).

subtopics.title: Responses to disease outbreaks

requirements.statement: Factors influencing an effective response to disease outbreaks: environmental, social, economic, political.

subtopics.title: Detailed specific examples of two contrasting countries’ responses to one pandemic since 2000:

requirements.statement: Similarities and differences in responses to the pandemic.
Factors (environmental, social, economic, political) influencing effective responses.

subtopics.title: Critical evaluation of a global programme to eradicate one pathogenic disease (any disease may be chosen): with reference to environmental, social, economic or political reasons for potential
